title: Read Tracked and Untracked State
summary: Use full and short status views to tell which files Git knows, which files are new, and which changes still need attention.

sketchBlocks.title: Decoding git status
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
The `git status` command is a powerful tool that provides a snapshot of the current state of your working directory and staging area. It helps you understand which changes are staged for the next commit, which are not, and which files are not being tracked by Git.

Worked example: Imagine you have a project with several files. After making some changes, you run `git status`. The output shows:

```
On branch main
Your branch is up to date with 'origin/main'.

Changes not staged for commit:
  (use "git add <file>..." to update what will be committed)
  (use "git restore <file>..." to discard changes in working directory)
	modified:   file1.txt

Untracked files:
  (use "git add <file>..." to include in what will be committed)
	file2.txt
```

This output tells you that `file1.txt` has been modified but not staged, and `file2.txt` is a new file that Git is not tracking yet. Understanding this output helps you decide your next steps, such as staging changes or adding new files to the repository.

sketchBlocks.title: Compact View with git status --short
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
For a more concise view of your repository's status, you can use `git status --short`. This command provides a brief overview of the changes in your working directory and staging area, using symbols to indicate the state of each file.

Worked example: After making changes to your project, you run `git status --short` and see:

```
 M file1.txt
?? file2.txt
```

In this output, `M` indicates that `file1.txt` has been modified, and `??` shows that `file2.txt` is untracked. This shorthand view is particularly useful when you want a quick overview without the detailed explanations provided by the full `git status` command.

sketchBlocks.title: Tracked vs. Untracked Files
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Understanding the difference between tracked and untracked files is crucial in Git. Tracked files are those that Git is aware of and are part of the repository's history. Untracked files, on the other hand, are new files that Git has not yet been told to track.

Worked example: Suppose you have a directory with several files. You run `git status` and see:

```
Untracked files:
  (use "git add <file>..." to include in what will be committed)
	newfile.txt
```

This indicates that `newfile.txt` is not yet tracked by Git. To start tracking it, you would use `git add newfile.txt`. Once added, it becomes a tracked file and will be included in future commits.

sketchBlocks.title: Applying git status in Real Projects
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Using `git status` effectively can prevent many common mistakes in version control. It helps ensure that you are aware of all changes before committing, reducing the risk of accidentally omitting important updates or including unwanted changes.

Worked example: You're working on a project and have made several changes. Before committing, you run `git status` to review the current state. You notice a file you forgot to stage. By catching this oversight, you can stage the file and ensure it is included in your next commit, maintaining a complete and accurate project history.

quizDraft.title: Practice with git status
quizDraft.prompt: Initialize a new Git repository and create two files: `tracked.txt` and `untracked.txt`. Add `tracked.txt` to the staging area. Use `git status` to verify the state of your repository. What does the output show?
quizDraft.starterCode:
# Initialize a new Git repository
# Create two files: tracked.txt and untracked.txt
# Add tracked.txt to the staging area
# Use git status to verify the state

starterFiles.content:
# Initialize a new Git repository
# Create two files: tracked.txt and untracked.txt
# Add tracked.txt to the staging area
# Use git status to verify the state

solutionFiles.content:
git init
touch tracked.txt untracked.txt
git add tracked.txt
git status

quizDraft.solutionCode:
git init
touch tracked.txt untracked.txt
git add tracked.txt
git status
quizDraft.hint: Remember to initialize the repository and add only one file to the staging area.

help.concept: The `git status` command shows the state of the working directory and staging area.
help.hint_2: Add `tracked.txt` to the staging area using `git add tracked.txt`.

quizDraft.title: Using git status --short
quizDraft.prompt: Create a new Git repository and two files: `fileA.txt` and `fileB.txt`. Modify `fileA.txt` and leave `fileB.txt` untracked. Use `git status --short` to check the status. What does the output show?
quizDraft.starterCode:
# Initialize a new Git repository
# Create two files: fileA.txt and fileB.txt
# Modify fileA.txt
# Use git status --short to check the status

starterFiles.content:
# Initialize a new Git repository
# Create two files: fileA.txt and fileB.txt
# Modify fileA.txt
# Use git status --short to check the status

solutionFiles.content:
git init
touch fileA.txt fileB.txt
echo 'modification' > fileA.txt
git status --short

quizDraft.solutionCode:
git init
touch fileA.txt fileB.txt
echo 'modification' > fileA.txt
git status --short
quizDraft.hint: Use `git status --short` to see a concise view of the changes.

help.concept: `git status --short` provides a brief overview of changes using symbols.
help.hint_1: Initialize the repository and create the files.
help.hint_2: Modify `fileA.txt` and check the status with `git status --short`.

quizDraft.title: Distinguishing Tracked and Untracked Files
quizDraft.prompt: Initialize a Git repository and create a file `example.txt`. Do not add it to the staging area. Use `git status` to determine if the file is tracked or untracked.
quizDraft.starterCode:
# Initialize a new Git repository
# Create a file: example.txt
# Use git status to check if it's tracked or untracked

starterFiles.content:
# Initialize a new Git repository
# Create a file: example.txt
# Use git status to check if it's tracked or untracked

solutionFiles.content:
git init
touch example.txt
git status

quizDraft.solutionCode:
git init
touch example.txt
git status
quizDraft.hint: Files not added to the staging area remain untracked.

help.concept: Untracked files are those not yet added to the staging area.
help.hint_1: Initialize the repository and create the file.
help.hint_2: Use `git status` to see the file's status.

quizDraft.title: Practical Use of git status
quizDraft.prompt: You have a Git repository with several changes. Create a file `important.txt` and modify an existing file `notes.txt`. Use `git status` to ensure all changes are accounted for before committing.
quizDraft.starterCode:
# Assume a Git repository with changes
# Create a file: important.txt
# Modify an existing file: notes.txt
# Use git status to ensure all changes are accounted for

starterFiles.content:
# Assume a Git repository with changes
# Create a file: important.txt
# Modify an existing file: notes.txt
# Use git status to ensure all changes are accounted for

solutionFiles.content:
touch important.txt
echo 'update' >> notes.txt
git status

quizDraft.solutionCode:
touch important.txt
echo 'update' >> notes.txt
git status
quizDraft.hint: Check the status to see which files are modified or untracked.

help.concept: `git status` helps ensure all changes are accounted for before committing.
help.hint_1: Create and modify the files as described.
help.hint_2: Use `git status` to verify the changes.

quizDraft.title: Purpose of git status
quizDraft.prompt: What is the primary purpose of the `git status` command?
options: To commit changes to the repository
To show the current branch and changes
To delete files from the repository
quizDraft.hint: Think about what information `git status` provides about your repository.

help.concept: `git status` provides information about the current state of the working directory and staging area.
help.hint_1: It shows which changes are staged, unstaged, or untracked.
help.hint_2: It helps you understand what will be included in the next commit.

quizDraft.title: Understanding git status --short
quizDraft.prompt: What does the `??` symbol indicate in the output of `git status --short`?
quizDraft.hint: Consider what `??` might mean about a file's status in the repository.

help.concept: The `??` symbol indicates untracked files in `git status --short`.
help.hint_1: Untracked files are those not yet added to the staging area.
help.hint_2: `??` means the file is new and not tracked by Git.

quizDraft.title: Components of git status output
quizDraft.prompt: Which of the following are components of the `git status` output? Select all that apply.
quizDraft.hint: Think about the different sections `git status` might show.

help.concept: `git status` shows the current branch, staged changes, unstaged changes, and untracked files.
help.hint_1: It provides a comprehensive view of the repository's state.
help.hint_2: Consider what information is necessary to understand the current state of your project.

quizDraft.title: Order of git status sections
quizDraft.prompt: Arrange the sections of the `git status` output in the correct order.
quizDraft.hint: Consider the logical flow of information in `git status`.

help.concept: `git status` typically shows the current branch, followed by staged changes, unstaged changes, and untracked files.
help.hint_1: Think about what information is most immediately relevant.
help.hint_2: The order reflects the progression from tracked to untracked changes.

quizDraft.title: Symbol for modified files
quizDraft.prompt: In `git status --short`, the symbol for a modified file is `[blank1]`.
quizDraft.template: In `git status --short`, the symbol for a modified file is `[blank1]`.
quizDraft.hint: Think about the symbol used to indicate modifications.

help.concept: The `the missing term` symbol indicates a modified file in `git status --short`.
help.hint_2: This symbol is used for files that have been changed but not staged.

quizDraft.title: Symbol for untracked files
quizDraft.prompt: In `git status --short`, the symbol for an untracked file is `[blank1]`.
quizDraft.template: In `git status --short`, the symbol for an untracked file is `[blank1]`.
quizDraft.hint: Consider the symbol used for files not yet added to the repository.

help.concept: The `??` symbol indicates an untracked file in `git status --short`.
help.hint_2: This symbol is used for files that are new and not tracked by Git.
